text:
Course: CAP4053 | Instructor: Johnathan Mell | Section: Contact Information | After class or by appointment
Email: Johnathan.Mell@ucf.edu
Student Learning Outcomes
All outcomes apply to 5937 (graduate) students. Outcomes marked with a (G) only apply to
graduate students, and the remainder make up the 4053 (undergraduate) list.
Learn how to make video games
(G) Learn how to lead a video game design team
Identify and explain some areas of game programming that AI programmers handle that are not
traditionally defined by academia as AI
Explain what makes some Game AI “good” and other Game AI “bad”
Use the Unity game engine and tutorials to create a simple game with AI, input and other interactive
features
Engage with your peers on Discord and communicate professionally about games
Use basic vector operations to solve basic AI problems such as visibility cone computations
Demonstrate understanding of the basic process of programming and how time and movement work
in real-time systems
(G) Deploy multiple AI systems that serve different functions within your game
Identify and explain methods by which modern games implement decision making
Demonstrate understanding of various pathfinding algorithms used in modern games
Explain basic steering of entities, and how they achieve their desired effects
Explain how game developers use focus testing to improve their AI (G) and provide detailed analysis
of that testing

text:
Course: CAP4053 | Instructor: Johnathan Mell | Section: Course Description (Part 1) | CAP 4053 ENGR-COMP SCI 3(3,0)AI for Game Programming: PR: Complete all of the followingEarn a
minimum grade of C in each of the following: COP3502C - Computer Science I (3)or C.I. Surveys cutting-
edge AI techniques for video games and board games and contrasts them with more traditional
approaches. Spr
This is a High-Impact Course and will be noted as such on your transcript (undergrad only)!
Classroom: <TBD>
Course Modality: In-person*
We will use Discord extensively
We will use Zoom occasionally. See homepage for individual section links.
*see below regarding illness and recorded lectures
Past game streams are available on YouTube
This a project-based course in which small groups will build a game, learning a great deal about how to
do so in the process. This includes:
learning quite a bit about programming in the practical sense
learning both AI and “traditional” techniques for creating video games
learning about what games ARE, and how to formally talk about them
learning how to make games fun
learning a small amount about the business and economics of game-making
Some groups will include a graduate student "team lead". This team lead will be responsible for project
managing the course, working with the other team members to deploy a more complex and ambitious
project. This means as a graduate student, you will have additional responsibilities to manage your team,
and the overall project will benefit from your involvement (although the expectations for the whole team
will be commensurately higher).
The vast majority of your grade in this course is made up of several checkpoints in making a game. Doing
a good job with making your game will mean you are almost certain to pass the course. Hopefully, you
will also find the experience enjoyable!

Course: CAP4053 | Instructor: Johnathan Mell | Section: Course Description (Part 2) | Note: this class is exam-lite, but requires a substantial amount of time outside class. Especially if you
are simultaneously enrolled in Senior Design, this can be daunting! Start early, and have regular meetings,
with at least some in-person.
Required Course Materials and Resources
GameAI Pro
Notes: Optional resource! GameAI Pro is a reference guide, and generally I will not assign readings from
it. It should be used to help guide your development, and reading it is recommended to help elaborate on
lecture content.
Online Access: https://www.gameaipro.com/
